summary: Joel Spolsky criticizes the Netscape 6.0 team for removing the Alt+Left keyboard shortcut for navigating back, a convention millions of users already learned. He argues that deviating from established UI conventions out of ideological opposition to Microsoft is self-defeating and harms users. The post illustrates a broader principle: user expectations trump designer preferences.
key_points: Established keyboard shortcuts become user expectations that software should respect regardless of origin
Rejecting UI conventions from competitors out of ideology hurts your own users, not the competitor
Smugness about design principles is no substitute for usability
money_quotes: Even if you think that Alt+Left is not a good shortcut key for 'Back', there are literally millions of people out there who will try to use Alt+Left to go back.

If you refuse to do it on some general religious principle that Bill Gates is the evil Smurf Gargamel, then you are just gratuitously ruining your program so that you can feel smug and self-satisfied, and your users will not thank you for it.
key_insight: Breaking established UI conventions to spite a competitor punishes your own users, not the competitor.
